speaker: interviewer
text: Interviews with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape 3. [? Lucek, ?] you were talking about changes occurring as-- as Hitler came to power. Were you aware around '38 and the beginning of '39 about what was occurring in Germany to Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew from papers. And we had some friends and relatives who lived in Germany. We have a couple of uncles, and-- but they-- they say they force-- they went to America because they just got somehow papers. And they were Ukrainian born. And the papers was full of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were--

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, that means we have this-- these two Jewish papers in Polish language. And they were full of them, you see. That means we just had history in the making every day. And you see, we knew about Kristallnacht and other things. But we sort of didn't expect that it's going too far you see. We-- we were the opinions that Hitler used the Jews, you see, as voting, you see, power. And then, it would be stupid to liquidate, you see, people who were very useful. Because, as in the first World War, Jews were helping Hindenburg and the Kaiser. And we thought, well, he-- he achieved his position. And so then, and, you see, sort of we-- we read in the paper about Austria. And so, that's-- and, you see, and then it came that, in 1939, or '38, you see, we were actually the Poles that had helping Hitler. Because when Hitler picked up the Sudets, the Poles-- picked up a bit of Silesia too. And they sort of declared the war to Lithuania because Lithuania was asking for the return of the City of Vilna, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent an ultimatum to-- that they have to may-- start diplomatic relationship, et cetera. And they, in Poland, Warsaw, they were creating a demonstration and, you see, there was a general, which may go in. There was leader, lead us against the Czechs. And then, lead us against the Lithuanians. And that was a war, and the Germans were very good friends. You see? And, you see, that means they started against the Jews, you see, the first, as a lot of Jews were communist.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, belong to the Communist Party. And Communist Party was not allowed to be a member of the government. [? You see, ?] before Hitler, there were a lot of Jews jailed. You see, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this is around 1938, 1939. Were you still living at home then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- let me-- I-- I had a bachelor room flat. You see, let me-- and I think in '38 I moved out to be nearer to my work and to have a little bit of more freedom. You know, and but you see, we were coming to Mother because she cooked good soups and everything, you know. And at that time, in 1939, we sort of, well, life was very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't-- you weren't concerned at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, and I wasn't, you see, at least in Gdansk, Danzig, which was a free city, but that was taken over by the-- [INAUDIBLE]. The municipality voted Nazis also. So we had a couple of customers, you know, and that left for South America. And they, of course, they left through Warsaw. And they visited us. And I was sitting with my two brothers. That Mr. Henry was the boss or a partner to the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Romik ?] and I, he was looking after the sales. I was looking after the purchases and sampling. And we ask-- [? Romik ?] ask Henry tell me, what about you going away? So he said, look, if I have here a million zlotys, I am a boss. I am a secretary of the thing, and the representative of [? Coty. ?] I live in luxury or something. When I go overseas, the million will become a 100,000, [? 1,100, ?] of whatever it is. And in America, there's a lot of millionaires or something. Who will-- who [? have ?] [? thinking ?] or so. I will-- we-- we have no option. And say-- say war or not war, we were, at that time, we were very, very much confused. But we hoped that, you see, that means to kill 3 1/2 million Jews, you have to bury them. You see, technically, we did-- we think it wasn't possible. Who'll survive? We'll survive. Are you saying that you-- you'd heard somewhere that-- that this was a possibility?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no possibility. We didn't have the people were taken to labor camp. People were dying early. People were, you see, there was shortage of medicine. And let's take a chance and survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you on September the 1st when war broke out in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, you see, we were-- were in-- in August we were on holiday, my wife and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in May, 7th of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39-- we had a short honeymoon, so we took another holiday in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leonia, Lola.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-O-L-A. That's what she was called. Leonia. And you see, we had a short holiday. So in September we took a holiday down in [? Pennine. ?] That was a mountain pass, you see, with a river going through it. And it was very beautiful. There were mineral waters. There were all-- all sorts of things. And you see, there were peasant [? dinners ?] et cetera. We had a-- a very good time. And we took an excursion from [? Croshenko ?] to Srebenica that [? leads ?] from one township to another along with the river [? Donets. ?] And this was, you see, maybe more crooked like the other, you know. And it was a back formed on four-- from four locks. And it took four people and a navigator with a very, very long ore, you see. And when we came to the end, it took, you see, a whole day over the river. And it-- it was beautiful. When we came to the end, we found out there's a mobilization. There was a mobilization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we met another couple from Krakow. And we have to go home. Was impossible to get a bus or something. So in partnership, we hired a taxi. We went to Krakow. See--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the couple?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I remember that they were [? ferriers. ?] And they were driving with us along the mountain pass, you see, amongst the Jewish villages to buy sheepskin. Because that was the center of cheap sheepskin. And you see, that was a very good experience because we saw so many township. And of course, we saw shops, you know, that may-- so on one side was a Jewish bar or something, inn. And on this other side there was a Christian firm you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Krakow for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the same day, we went to the railway station. They went home. And they let us out on-- at the railway station on the [INAUDIBLE] Street. And we were-- waited for something, and we got ourselves into a-- so a very packed train going up north. And took us two days or so to come to Warsaw. We see on the way-- we saw that most of the tracks were busy with army, et cetera. And we came back, you see, that is end of August. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to the office, and [? Helena ?] wasn't [INAUDIBLE] [? little ?] for-- [? Helena-- ?] Lola, my wife, [INAUDIBLE]. We went to work for a day. And then, the next day, the 1st of [INAUDIBLE], we were in our little flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day what--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the 1st of September, we were in our little flat and kind of ready to work. And as usually, [? Bessarabia ?] was a [INAUDIBLE] was, for a few years, but [INAUDIBLE] I, we had a-- a good set. And we opened the set, and a musical that makes military marches one after the other. And in between the marches, there was a proclamation of the president of the commonwealth of this Polish Republic that this morning the so and so army invaded our Poland, which put us in state of war, which I [? state ?] in the name of God and history, et cetera, et cetera. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: --there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a very big mix up. I went to the office. And we didn't know what to do. But you see, let me-- the two partners were away. You see, one went for the exhibition in America and stayed back-- didn't come back to Warsaw. And the other partner was in France, and somehow he got to America. And Henry was the only one with-- with [? Ronald, ?] or Robin and I, and-- and the accountant. And he said, you see, there with so much money in the cash, and we divided among the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amongst your workers, amongst the workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, the office workers. The factory was going. You see? And-- and we took, you see, parcels home. Toothpaste, face powder, lotions, the cologne, et cetera, et cetera. And we had on the-- on the customs 100 kilo of [? Coty's ?] perfume. 100 kilos could make up, you see, 10,000 little bottles. And this-- this was stopped because we didn't have the papers, the clearing papers. Because if you wanted to import, you had to export. And you were buying export papers to clear your imports. But I went to the customs, and I got the 100 kilos out without anything. You see, they were in aluminum, you see, sort of containers. And the-- the factory started working, bottling, et cetera. And the-- you see, that means we did whatever we could do. And was hard with transport to work, but somehow we managed. You see, Lola was in walking distance to her office, and-- but I always took a tram and a bus. And somehow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --well, it-- it was a very, very short story, you see, [? I mean ?] seven day-- on the seventh day, you see, the-- the [? Ranya ?] was yelling of victories at so and so. And on the seventh day there was a-- a advice that all able men should leave Warsaw and go east, you see. And we-- and so, we were sitting, so what-- how-- how-- how are we going east?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why should we walk? Henry had a Buick, the company car. So we said instead of going on the seventh, I don't know, sixth or the seventh at night, we will start in the morning. We have a car, and we have plenty of petrol. Well, In the meantime an officer came with a requisition card and took away the car. So we left back in Warsaw all during the invasion of Warsaw, you see, it wasn't a pleasant time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We listened to the radio. Three days later America declared-- England declared war. The French declared war. And they started to play French marches and English marches. And-- and we were in a mood that-- that the English and the-- and the French are just going back knocking Hitler. And we will come back. Well, nothing came through, you see. That makes 28 days of invasion. And halfway through, the Russians came from the east because they had an agreement to divide Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the moment you-- you saw-- saw the first German soldier coming into Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I that it-- it was on the 28th of September, you see. Let me say the president of the-- of town, the-- you see, he surrendered. But the Germans came two days later. In between, they came, you see, the robbers. They were, you see, robbing the shops and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poles, Jews, everybody-- hooligans, you see. And they were running with sort of push trolleys, you see, you know, and selling. You see, and you could buy in the street whatever it is. And everybody was, you see, happy to buy from the robbers something. And the telephones there were still working. And, you see, that my big brother, the-- the rich one, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told him that somebody wants to sell a-- a case of sardines. Because everybody wanted, you see, over the occupation to have something. So I said, what kind of brand? Is it [? Luis Cano? ?] I told him, you old, stupid bastard you're going to buy French-- French sardines. And-- and ordinary sardines, I-- I've not gotten-- you got used to it with-- with your father. You're not a sort of a-- that people here are crazy. Anyway, you see, we stayed back. And we still, you see, by that time, the-- what's it called? Where it's no-- or when armor-- army leaves and the other comes, you see. There is the robbery time et cetera. They robbed the cellar of the factory of a-- a shipment of coconut butter, which we used for cosmetics.

speaker: interviewee
text: But everything was all right. And we were sort of thinking what to do, and how to arrange, and how to expend the-- expend the dollar, you know. The people making sausages and black market buying butter, et cetera. All sorts of food, and a few-- a week or so later, the people who ran away on the 7th of September started coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- they were coming back, and most-- the one we knew-- coming back to pick up families. And they were telling stories about Russia. And they're so good, and they're so nice. And, you see, not like Germans. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Robin was playing-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] poker with a friend who had a wholesale bakery supplies. And another boy was in the same sort of poker school. And they had the idea that we'd better go because, also, an uncle of my wife came back to pick up his family who-- which he never did. But he had the intention-- and telling the stories that because we can sell watches, we can make money, et cetera. And a lot of people, there was an industry of smuggling stuff from there to there because the Germans wanted watches. And they-- and you can buy cheap Polish zlotys over there, which were still good in the-- in thing. So he said, and we took up, you see, that these two boys, you see, [? they're ?] from this poker school with the wife-- my wife, and myself, and a few friends when he said that he can take 12 people and bring all the stuff, what you can sell. And because we had-- at home, we had these little bottles of perfume or something which were valuable. We sort of bought a lot of cheap watches, et cetera, you know. And, you see, we went to this flat, and this driver of Mr. Tishler--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Mr. Tishler?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the one who was the wholesaler of baker supplies, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is-- was he the one who was organizing this?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't go, but his driver who was driving his van to the bakers or something, you know, delivery van-- he started because, at that time, they-- it became an industry. You see, the people who went there and back, and smuggling, and they were smuggling people too. So he said that he is all right, you see, he is organized. And the Germans are bribed, and the Russian are bribed. And they-- and be-- it was some sort of early November [? because ?] a month after. And he was to pick us up. And we all came here, and Robin wasn't in because he was to come too, and he didn't. Because he was stopped somewhere and taken with other people to clean the lavatory or something, you see, because the German [? command ?] were picking up people from the street out of the trams, you see, as cleaners.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they released him, and he didn't come yet. And the fellow, the driver, came up. Are-- are you all ready? Yes. So I'll be back in five minutes. So all of a sudden we see the SS people coming up stairs. And we were in a big mess. And they were asking, where is Robin Zylberman? And he wasn't in. I said, we don't know. Oh, well, he picked-- they picked up all the jewelry and the fur coats, you see. That means for this trip, I was wearing two suits and a fur coat, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: That [INAUDIBLE] this was a sort of a necessity because [INAUDIBLE]. And the fur coats and the things-- and lucky we had it because we took it down to them, the [INAUDIBLE] [? SS, ?] you see. And if they would arrest us, they would have to take all the stuff to the headquarters. Apparently, they took it for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I brought down the last parcel, he said, [GERMAN]. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go away Jew. And you see, so-- so we were free, but they had all our addresses and everything. And we were afraid. So I went to-- to sleep to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Lola with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, all the time. And we-- and-- and in the meantime, [? Rom ?] and Robin came back and told us that-- that-- that had-- he had to clean a lavatory without a brush. And it's no business to stay with the Germans, et cetera. [INAUDIBLE] so, you see, the industry, there were a lot of salesmen. And the smuggling industry east-- well, east and westwards-- was blooming, you see. And there were hundreds of smugglers, you see, and friends. Well, I'll take you over. We took-- that makes a smaller group. We went to-- in another goods truck, covered, you see, with some hay. We went towards [NON-ENGLISH], which was a border town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So had-- could you just once describe again how you-- you went to this town? What was the mode of transport?

speaker: interviewee
text: A truck a goods truck, which on top carried merchandise, and we were sort of hidden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my wife, myself, my brother, my sister-in-law, and a couple of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak to your parents before you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Parents said, you young people, you go. Because the message was that in Russia is-- there's no anti-semitism, et cetera. And what do they want from old people? We will survive or not they said. Well, the second trip was stopped because it was the 10th of November, I remember. And a fellow on a bike stopped the [INAUDIBLE] truck. And so, and he said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews run away. You see, why? Because tomorrow is the 11th of November, and they already-- the German already took a group of people and executed it on the marketplace or something. We went back. And then we approached another smuggler. And this time, we went to a place called Mordy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-R-D-Y. And we went over there, I remember, you see, we were there in a sort of back yard of a house. And I remember my mother came and-- and brought some cheesecakes that smelt that [? oil, ?] you see, she-- she dressed with a [? sheet ?] hung over her head like a shiksa, you see, like a peasant. And gave us a cake, and we went-- and we came. It was sort of a nightfall because it was already the 13th of-- almost-- of November. And we came to an inn. And from that there were a few people who put us in a van, maybe 20 people from different buses on a big fisherman's boat. And we crossed a river.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which river was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bug, B-U-G, which was the border between, I think the Russian zone and the German zone. And they left us on the bank, on the other bank, on the Russian bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we paid a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to take a break there and change tapes.
